scenes.title: Vector Addition: $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{c}$
scenes.description: The sum of two vectors $\vec{a} = (2, 1, 0)$ and $\vec{b} = (1, 2, 1)$ is found by placing $\vec{b}$ at the tip of $\vec{a}$. The resultant $\vec{c} = (3, 3, 1)$ goes from the origin to the tip of $\vec{b}$.
scenes.prompt:
You are teaching vector addition. Follow these guidelines for this scene:

## Interactive Follow-Up Examples
When the student asks to explore further, build slider-driven scenes:
- Let them adjust individual components of a and b (sliders for ax, ay, az, bx, by, bz)
- Show the parallelogram updating in real time with an animated_polygon
- Display the magnitude of the resultant as a text label that updates

## Related Topics to Suggest
After the student understands addition, naturally suggest:
1. **Vector subtraction** — "Want to see what happens when we subtract b from a? The difference vector points from tip to tip."
2. **Scalar multiplication** — "Curious what happens when you scale a vector by a number? We can explore stretching and flipping."
3. **Linear combinations** — "Ready for the big picture? Any vector in the plane can be written as a combination of two independent vectors."

## Guided Exploration Prompts
Encourage the student to discover these insights:
- "What happens when the two vectors are parallel? Try setting b proportional to a."
- "What if the vectors point in opposite directions? Watch the resultant shrink."
- "Can you make the resultant land exactly on the x-axis? What constraint does that put on the y-components?"
- "Notice that |a + b| is usually less than |a| + |b| — when does equality hold?"

## Step-by-Step Scene Patterns
When creating new explanatory scenes for follow-up questions:
1. Start with the two original vectors (keep colors consistent: red-orange for a, blue for b, gold for result)
2. Add one new concept per step (e.g., the translated copy, the parallelogram, the diagonal)
3. End with an interactive step that has sliders for the student to explore
4. Always include a markdown panel with the relevant formula and a worked example
scenes.markdown:
# Vector Addition

**Vector addition** combines two vectors to produce a third vector called the **resultant**.

## Definition

For vectors $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, b_3)$:

$$\vec{a} + \vec{b} = (a_1 + b_1, \; a_2 + b_2, \; a_3 + b_3)$$

Addition is performed **component-wise**.

## This Example

With $\vec{a} = (2, 1, 0)$ and $\vec{b} = (1, 2, 1)$:

$$\vec{c} = \vec{a} + \vec{b} = (2+1, \; 1+2, \; 0+1) = (3, 3, 1)$$

## The Parallelogram Law

Geometrically, vector addition follows the **tip-to-tail** method:

1. Place the tail of $\vec{b}$ at the tip of $\vec{a}$
2. The resultant $\vec{c}$ goes from the origin to the new tip

Notice the dashed path in the visualization: placing $\vec{b}$ first, then $\vec{a}$, arrives at the **same point** $C = (3, 3, 1)$. This forms a parallelogram.

## Magnitude

The magnitude of the resultant is:

$$|\vec{c}| = \sqrt{3^2 + 3^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{19} \approx 4.36$$

Note that $|\vec{c}| \leq |\vec{a}| + |\vec{b}|$ — this is the **triangle inequality**.

## Key Properties

- **Commutative**: $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{b} + \vec{a}$
- **Associative**: $(\vec{a} + \vec{b}) + \vec{c} = \vec{a} + (\vec{b} + \vec{c})$
- **Identity**: $\vec{a} + \vec{0} = \vec{a}$
- **Inverse**: $\vec{a} + (-\vec{a}) = \vec{0}$

> **Tip**: Rotate the view to see how the z-component of $\vec{b}$ lifts the resultant out of the xy-plane.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide isometric view — see all vectors in full 3D perspective

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view along Z — see vectors in the XY plane

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view looking down — see vectors projected onto the horizontal plane

views.description: Side view along X — see vectors in the YZ plane

views.name: Along $\vec{a}$
views.description: Looking along vector a — see the components perpendicular to a

views.name: Along $\vec{c}$
views.description: Looking along the resultant c — see c head-on

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{a}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{a} = (2, 1, 0)$ from the origin — it lies flat in the $xy$-plane.

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{b}$ (tip-to-tail)
steps.description: Place $\vec{b} = (1, 2, 1)$ at the tip of $\vec{a}$ — notice it lifts into the $z$-axis.

steps.title: Show resultant $\vec{c}$
steps.description: The resultant $\vec{c} = \vec{a} + \vec{b} = (3, 3, 1)$ goes from origin to the tip of $\vec{b}$.

steps.title: Show commutativity
steps.description: Reversing the order — $\vec{b}$ then $\vec{a}$ — arrives at the same point: $\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{b} + \vec{a}$.

steps.title: Interactive: adjust $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$
steps.description: Use the sliders to change each component of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$. The blue vector stays tip-to-tail on $\vec{a}$, and the gold resultant updates in real time.

scenes.title: Cross Product: $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$
scenes.description: The cross product of $\vec{a} = (2, 0, 0)$ and $\vec{b} = (1, 2, 0)$ yields $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (0, 0, 4)$, perpendicular to both vectors.
scenes.markdown:
# Cross Product

The **cross product** $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ produces a vector **perpendicular** to both input vectors.

## Definition

For vectors $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, b_3)$:

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ a_1 & a_2 & a_3 \\ b_1 & b_2 & b_3 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2)\hat{i} - (a_1 b_3 - a_3 b_1)\hat{j} + (a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)\hat{k}$$

## This Example

With $\vec{a} = (2, 0, 0)$ and $\vec{b} = (1, 2, 0)$:

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 2, \; 0 \cdot 1 - 2 \cdot 0, \; 2 \cdot 2 - 0 \cdot 1) = (0, 0, 4)$$

The result points along the **positive z-axis** (shown in gold).

## Geometric Meaning

- **Direction**: Perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, following the right-hand rule
- **Magnitude**: Equal to the area of the parallelogram formed by $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$

$$|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = |\vec{a}| \cdot |\vec{b}| \cdot \sin\theta = 4$$

The purple parallelogram in the visualization has area exactly $4$.

## Key Properties

- **Anti-commutative**: $\vec{b} \times \vec{a} = -(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})$
- **Not associative**: $(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times \vec{c} \neq \vec{a} \times (\vec{b} \times \vec{c})$
- **Distributive**: $\vec{a} \times (\vec{b} + \vec{c}) = \vec{a} \times \vec{b} + \vec{a} \times \vec{c}$

> **Tip**: Use the right-hand rule — curl fingers from $\vec{a}$ to $\vec{b}$, your thumb points in the direction of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$.

views.description: Wide view showing all three vectors and the cross product

views.description: Top view — see the plane spanned by a and b from above

views.description: Side view along X — see vectors in the YZ plane

views.name: Along $\vec{c}$
views.description: Looking along c — confirms c is perpendicular to the ab plane

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{a}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{a} = (2, 0, 0)$ along the $x$-axis.

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{b}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{b} = (1, 2, 0)$ in the $xy$-plane — both vectors are flat.

steps.title: Show parallelogram
steps.description: The parallelogram spanned by $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ has area $|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = 4$.

steps.title: Show $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$
steps.description: $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (0, 0, 4)$ — perpendicular to both, pointing up by the right-hand rule.

steps.title: Interactive: adjust $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$
steps.description: Use the sliders to change each component. The gold vector $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is always perpendicular to both — watch it flip when you reverse the orientation.

scenes.prompt: Teach cross product as orientation plus area: direction from right-hand rule, magnitude as parallelogram area, and sign flip under order reversal. Ask the learner to test near-parallel vectors and explain why the cross product shrinks toward zero.

scenes.title: Dot Product: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$
scenes.description: The dot product $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}|\cos\theta$ measures how much two vectors point in the same direction. The projection of $\vec{b}$ onto $\vec{a}$ has length $\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}|}$.
scenes.markdown:
# Dot Product

The **dot product** (or scalar product) takes two vectors and returns a **scalar**.

## Definition

For vectors $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, b_3)$:

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3$$

## Geometric Form

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = |\vec{a}| \cdot |\vec{b}| \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the vectors.

## This Example

With $\vec{a} = (3, 1, 0)$ and $\vec{b} = (1, 3, 1)$:

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 3 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 1 = 6$$

## Projection

The **scalar projection** of $\vec{b}$ onto $\vec{a}$ is:

$$\text{comp}_{\vec{a}} \vec{b} = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}|} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{10}} \approx 1.90$$

The **vector projection** is:

$$\text{proj}_{\vec{a}} \vec{b} = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}|^2} \vec{a} = \frac{6}{10}(3, 1, 0) = (1.8, 0.6, 0)$$

## Key Properties

- **Commutative**: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}$
- **Distributive**: $\vec{a} \cdot (\vec{b} + \vec{c}) = \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} + \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}$
- **Orthogonality test**: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0 \iff \vec{a} \perp \vec{b}$
- **Self dot product**: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} = |\vec{a}|^2$

> **Tip**: The dot product is positive when vectors point roughly the same way, zero when perpendicular, and negative when they point apart.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Isometric view — see both vectors and the projection clearly

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view — see the angle between the vectors

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view looking down — see vectors projected onto the horizontal plane

views.name: Right
views.description: Side view along X — see vectors in the YZ plane

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{a}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{a} = (3, 1, 0)$ from the origin along the $x$-axis.

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{b}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{b} = (1, 3, 1)$ — it points in a different direction from $\vec{a}$.

steps.title: Show projection of $\vec{b}$ onto $\vec{a}$
steps.description: The projection $\text{proj}_{\vec{a}}\vec{b} = \frac{\vec{a}\cdot\vec{b}}{|\vec{a}|^2}\vec{a} = (1.8, 0.6, 0)$ drops $\vec{b}$ perpendicularly onto $\vec{a}$.

steps.title: Show angle $\theta$
steps.description: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 6 = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}|\cos\theta$, giving $\theta \approx 63°$.

steps.title: Interactive: adjust $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$
steps.description: Drag the sliders to see how the projection changes. The gold vector is $\text{proj}_{\vec{a}}\vec{b}$ and the purple line shows the perpendicular rejection.

scenes.prompt: Teach dot product as alignment: positive, zero, negative based on angle. Connect algebraic form to projection and cosine, and prompt learners to test orthogonality and angle changes with sliders.

scenes.title: Scalar Multiplication: $c\vec{v}$
scenes.description: Multiplying a vector by a scalar $c$ stretches or shrinks it. Positive $c$ preserves direction, negative $c$ reverses it.
scenes.markdown:
# Scalar Multiplication

**Scalar multiplication** scales a vector's magnitude without changing its line of action (unless $c < 0$, which reverses direction).

## Definition

For a scalar $c$ and vector $\vec{v} = (v_1, v_2, v_3)$:

$$c\vec{v} = (cv_1, cv_2, cv_3)$$

## This Example

With $\vec{v} = (1, 1.5, 0.5)$:

- $2\vec{v} = (2, 3, 1)$ — twice as long, same direction
- $-1\vec{v} = (-1, -1.5, -0.5)$ — same length, opposite direction
- $0.5\vec{v} = (0.5, 0.75, 0.25)$ — half as long, same direction

## Geometric Meaning

- $|c| > 1$: vector gets **longer**
- $|c| < 1$: vector gets **shorter**
- $c > 0$: **same direction** as original
- $c < 0$: **opposite direction**
- $c = 0$: **zero vector**

## Magnitude

$$|c\vec{v}| = |c| \cdot |\vec{v}|$$

For $\vec{v} = (1, 1.5, 0.5)$: $|\vec{v}| = \sqrt{1 + 2.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{3.5} \approx 1.87$

## Key Properties

- **Associative**: $a(b\vec{v}) = (ab)\vec{v}$
- **Distributive over vector addition**: $c(\vec{u} + \vec{v}) = c\vec{u} + c\vec{v}$
- **Distributive over scalar addition**: $(a + b)\vec{v} = a\vec{v} + b\vec{v}$
- **Identity**: $1\vec{v} = \vec{v}$

> **Tip**: All scalar multiples of $\vec{v}$ lie along the same line through the origin. This line is the **span** of $\vec{v}$.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Isometric view — see the original and scaled vector together

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view along Z — see vectors in the XY plane

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view looking down — see vectors projected onto the horizontal plane

views.name: Right
views.description: Side view along X — see vectors in the YZ plane

steps.title: Original vector $\vec{v}$
steps.description: Start with $\vec{v} = (1, 1.5, 0.5)$, with magnitude $|\vec{v}| = \sqrt{3.5} \approx 1.87$.

steps.title: Double: $2\vec{v}$
steps.description: $2\vec{v} = (2, 3, 1)$ — twice the length, same direction as $\vec{v}$.

steps.title: Negate: $-\vec{v}$
steps.description: $-\vec{v} = (-1, -1.5, -0.5)$ — same length, opposite direction.

steps.title: Half: $0.5\vec{v}$
steps.description: $0.5\vec{v} = (0.5, 0.75, 0.25)$ — all scalar multiples lie on the span of $\vec{v}$.

scenes.prompt: Teach scalar multiplication as geometric scaling along one line: magnitude scales by |c| and sign controls direction reversal. Encourage quick experiments at c=0, c<0, and |c|>1.

scenes.title: Vector Subtraction: $\vec{a} - \vec{b}$
scenes.description: Subtracting $\vec{b}$ from $\vec{a}$ gives the vector from the tip of $\vec{b}$ to the tip of $\vec{a}$. Equivalently, $\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})$.
scenes.markdown:
# Vector Subtraction

**Vector subtraction** is defined as adding the negative:

$$\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})$$

## Definition

For vectors $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, b_3)$:

$$\vec{a} - \vec{b} = (a_1 - b_1, \; a_2 - b_2, \; a_3 - b_3)$$

## This Example

With $\vec{a} = (3, 1, 0)$ and $\vec{b} = (1, 3, 1)$:

$$\vec{a} - \vec{b} = (3-1, \; 1-3, \; 0-1) = (2, -2, -1)$$

## Geometric Interpretation

The difference vector $\vec{a} - \vec{b}$ points **from the tip of** $\vec{b}$ **to the tip of** $\vec{a}$.

This is extremely useful in physics and geometry:
- The displacement from point $B$ to point $A$
- The relative velocity of $A$ with respect to $B$
- The side of a triangle connecting two vertices

## Parallelogram Connection

When drawn from the origin, $\vec{a}$, $\vec{b}$, $\vec{a} + \vec{b}$, and $\vec{a} - \vec{b}$ form the two diagonals of a parallelogram.

## Key Properties

- **Not commutative**: $\vec{a} - \vec{b} \neq \vec{b} - \vec{a}$ (they are negatives of each other)
- **Self subtraction**: $\vec{a} - \vec{a} = \vec{0}$
- **Magnitude**: $|\vec{a} - \vec{b}|$ is the **distance** between points $A$ and $B$

$$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}| = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 1} = 3$$

> **Tip**: Think of subtraction as answering "how do I get from $B$ to $A$?"

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide view showing vectors a, b, and the difference a−b

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view — see the triangle formed by the vectors

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view looking down — see vectors projected onto the horizontal plane

views.name: Right
views.description: Side view along X — see vectors in the YZ plane

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{a}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{a} = (3, 1, 0)$ from the origin.

steps.title: Add vector $\vec{b}$
steps.description: Draw $\vec{b} = (1, 3, 1)$ — the tips of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are separated in space.

steps.title: Show $\vec{a} - \vec{b}$ from tip to tip
steps.description: $\vec{a} - \vec{b} = (2, -2, -1)$ points from the tip of $\vec{b}$ to the tip of $\vec{a}$.

steps.title: Show $-\vec{b}$ and addition path
steps.description: Equivalently, $\vec{a} - \vec{b} = \vec{a} + (-\vec{b})$ — add the negated vector tip-to-tail.

scenes.prompt: Teach subtraction as adding the opposite and as a displacement from b-tip to a-tip. Reinforce component formula a-b and ask learners to compare with a+(-b).

scenes.title: Span: Linear Combinations Fill Space
scenes.description: The span of a set of vectors is the collection of all their linear combinations. One vector spans a line, two independent vectors span a plane, and three independent vectors span all of $\mathbb{R}^3$.
scenes.markdown:
# Span of Vectors

The **span** of a set of vectors is the set of all possible **linear combinations**:

$$\text{span}(\vec{v}_1, \vec{v}_2, \dots, \vec{v}_k) = \{ c_1\vec{v}_1 + c_2\vec{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k\vec{v}_k \mid c_i \in \mathbb{R} \}$$

## Building Dimension

| Vectors | Span | Dimension |
|---------|------|-----------|
| $\vec{v}_1$ alone | A **line** through the origin | 1D |
| $\vec{v}_1, \vec{v}_2$ (independent) | A **plane** through the origin | 2D |
| $\vec{v}_1, \vec{v}_2, \vec{v}_3$ (independent) | All of $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 3D |

## Linear Independence

Vectors $\vec{v}_1, \dots, \vec{v}_k$ are **linearly independent** if the only solution to:

$$c_1\vec{v}_1 + c_2\vec{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k\vec{v}_k = \vec{0}$$

is $c_1 = c_2 = \cdots = c_k = 0$.

## This Example

$$\vec{v}_1 = (2, 1, 0), \quad \vec{v}_2 = (0, 1, 1.5), \quad \vec{v}_3 = (1, 0, 2)$$

### Determinant Test

$$\det \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.5 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = 2(2 - 0) - 0 + 1(1.5 - 0) = 5.5 \neq 0$$

Since the determinant is non-zero, the three vectors are **linearly independent** and span all of $\mathbb{R}^3$.

## Key Insight

Every vector in $\mathbb{R}^3$ can be written **uniquely** as a linear combination of three linearly independent vectors — they form a **basis**.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Wide view showing how linear combinations fill space

views.name: Front
views.description: Front view along Z — see vectors in the XY plane

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view — see the span from directly above

views.name: Right
views.description: Side view along X — see vectors in the YZ plane

steps.title: Draw $\vec{v}_1$
steps.description: Start with $\vec{v}_1 = (2, 1, 0)$ from the origin — it lies in the $xy$-plane.

steps.title: Span of one vector: a line
steps.description: All scalar multiples $c_1 \vec{v}_1$ trace out a line through the origin — a 1D subspace.

steps.title: Add $\vec{v}_2$
steps.description: $\vec{v}_2 = (0, 1, 1.5)$ points out of the $xy$-plane — it is linearly independent from $\vec{v}_1$.

steps.title: Sweeping the plane
steps.description: The animated vector traces $c_1 \vec{v}_1 + c_2 \vec{v}_2$ — all linear combinations of two independent vectors fill a plane. Adjust $r$ to change the sweep radius.

steps.title: Sample combinations
steps.description: Concrete examples: each point is a specific $c_1 \vec{v}_1 + c_2 \vec{v}_2$ lying in the plane.

steps.title: Add $\vec{v}_3$ (off the plane)
steps.description: $\vec{v}_3 = (1, 0, 2)$ does not lie in $\text{span}(\vec{v}_1, \vec{v}_2)$ — it points out of the plane, adding a third dimension.

steps.title: Three vectors span $\mathbb{R}^3$
steps.description: With $\det \neq 0$, every point in 3D space is reachable. Sample combinations fill the volume.

scenes.prompt: Teach span as the set of all linear combinations. Emphasize one vector gives a line, two independent vectors give a plane, and adding a non-coplanar third vector fills 3D space.
